schema_json.description: A question generated by a persona, with decomposed search queries.

search_queries.description: 2-3 web search queries to find answer material
search_queries.title: Search Queries

schema_json.title: StormQuestion

prompt:
You are Dr. Eleanor Whitfield, an expert in Senior health economist at the London School of Economics Personal Social Services Research Unit, specializing in decision-analytic cost-effectiveness modeling of lifestyle interventions for type 2 diabetes and metabolic syndrome prevention. Builds Markov and microsimulation models of QALY and cost-of-illness trajectories using NICE reference case methods, and has published budget-impact analyses of lifestyle programs versus semaglutide for the NHS..

You are conducting a research interview about:
RESEARCH QUESTION: What are the proven health benefits and risks of intermittent fasting in adults with metabolic syndrome?

Your perspective: Economic
Your focus area: She would focus on the cost-effectiveness gap between IF and the standard of care: ICER per QALY gained, 10-year budget impact versus no-intervention and versus GLP-1 agonists, the economic value of modest but durable HbA1c reductions across a population at risk, and the real-world economics of free vs subscription IF tools. She would flag the near-absence of IF-specific cost-effectiveness data as a decision-making obstacle for payers.

PREVIOUS CONVERSATION ROUNDS:
Round 1:
  Q: What is the evidence base on the cost-effectiveness of intermittent fasting as an intervention for metabolic syndrome — specifically, have any published economic evaluations quantified the incremental
  A: The search results recover ONE published IF-specific economic evaluation plus several adjacent references. Sources 1, 3, and 5 (PMC12599020, ResearchGate, PubMed 41214519) all describe the same study: a trial-based analysis and lifetime model-based analysis of the cost-effectiveness of a one-year mo
Round 2:
  Q: Round 1 identified that the only published IF-specific economic evaluation is the trial-based and lifetime model-based cost-utility analysis of a monthly 5-day fasting-mimicking diet (FMD) programme i
  A: The search results provide partial specifics on the FMD-in-T2D cost-utility study. Source 2 (ScienceDirect, doi S1098301524028468) and Source 9 (PubMed 39343090) — the same paper with two links — disclose the key effectiveness claims: the FMD programme was associated with '11.4% less diabetes compli

This is the THIRD question. Look for contradictions, nuances, or under-explored aspects. Ask about methodology, data quality, or alternative interpretations.

Generate:
1. ONE focused, specific research question from your expert perspective. Do NOT repeat questions already asked. The question should be answerable through web/academic search — avoid purely philosophical or speculative questions.
2. Exactly 5 web search queries that would help find the answer. Use precise, search-engine-friendly language. Include at least one query with specific technical terms or named entities.

Return a JSON object with 'question', 'search_queries', and 'perspective' fields.
